summary: Near‑perfect Betty Cantor‑Jackson soundboard captures a May ’77 masterpiece – dazzling Half‑Step, scorching Scarlet > Fire and an epic Estimated Prophet → He’s Gone → The Other One run.
review: The show is consistently lauded for its tight playing and inspired improvisations, especially the second‑set “dream set” (Scarlet > Fire, Estimated Prophet, He’s Gone, The Other One, Wharf Rat, Wheel).  The SBD sounds crystal‑clear with studio‑board fidelity; a few listeners note occasional low volume or slight muddiness but overall it is regarded as one of the best‑recorded 1977 performances.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 80 % of reviews are 4–5 stars and praise both performance and sound quality; the few minor criticisms do not outweigh the overwhelming enthusiasm.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Betty Cantor‑Jackson board, crisp, balanced mix with superb instrument separation.  A handful of comments mention slightly low volume or occasional mud, but overall the audio is described as “near album quality” and “the best SBD on the site”.

poor_songs: Estimated > He’s Gone (some felt it let down the flow)
Scarlet > Fire (a few noted it sounded quiet)
show_quality.setlist_flow: First set is praised for its strong openers (Half‑Step, Jack Straw, Peggy‑O) and solid mid‑tempo numbers; the second set is universally described as a “dream set”, with seamless segues and high‑energy improvisations that keep the momentum soaring.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly highlighted for incendiary guitar work – especially on Half‑Step, Estimated Prophet, He’s Gone and The Other One; reviewers call his playing “otherworldly” and “possessed”.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass praised for a funky line on Scarlet and overall solid foundation throughout the set.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith (and later Brent) noted for tasteful piano/organ work; most reviewers find the keyboard texture supportive, with only occasional remarks about later‑era “jacked up” tones.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill (and Mickey on drum solo spots) receive kudos for tight, energetic playing; one reviewer likens the drumming to a Gene Krupa/Buddy Rich style.
